quotes.text: There has been a question as to what Ezekiel refers to as the "thirtieth year." Traditional Jewish commentaries say it was the thirtieth year of the jubilee, but do not say when the last jubilee year had occurred. Thirty years from 625 B.C., the beginning of the reign of Nebopolassar, father of Nebuchadnezzar, gives the date 595 B.C. That would be about the same as the fifth year of Jehoiachin's captivity and would be a reasonable interpretation. The place, the river Chebar, could have been the Euphrates by its Babylonian name-Naru Kabari. However, some think it was a canal that connected the Euphrates and the Tigris near their confluence. We do not have record of any other Hebrew prophets called to the prophetic mission while living outside the land of Israel.
quotes.speaker: Ellis Rasmussen
quotes.attr: Ellis Rasmussen, Introduction to the Old Testament 2:84

notes: the Chaldeans by the river Chebar; and the hand of the LORD was there upon him.

The words wind, tempest, or storm would better fit the meaning intended in Ezekiel 1:4. A wind that revolves on its own axis with great rapidity is not what is meant by the Hebrew word translated "whirlwind"; rather, the idea of a furious or powerful wind is what was intended (see Merrill F. Unger, Unger's Bible Dictionary, s.v. "whirlwind"). The metaphor signifies the power of God. For instance, the power of God's presence was indicated to Job through allusion to a whirlwind (see Job 38:1). When the Lord poured out his Spirit with great power at the dedication of the Kirtland Temple in this dispensation, "a noise was heard like the sound of a rushing mighty wind, which filled the Temple" (History of the Church, 2:428).

quotes.text: The Prophet Joseph Smith said: "I make this broad declaration, that whenever God gives a vision of an image, or beast, or figure of any kind, He always holds Himself responsible to give a revelation or interpretation of the meaning thereof, otherwise we are not responsible or accountable for our belief in it. Don't be afraid of being damned for not knowing the meaning of a vision or figure, if God has not given a revelation or interpretation of the subject.
quotes.speaker: Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith
quotes.source: Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith
quotes.attr: Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith, p. 291

quotes.text: There is scarcely anything that gives a higher lustre than highly polished or burnished brass (Clarke, Bible Commentary, 4:425) In the authorized version it is translated as amber and represents beauty and glory

notes: today and forever; and... his course is...without variation" (Lectures on Faith, 3:15). All beings who serve him and follow the dictates of his spirit also are undeviating, but go where he leads them and nowhere else. (see also verses 20-21).

They went straight forward in the direction prompted by the spirit. God "changes not, neither is there variableness with him; but...he is the same from everlasting to everlasting, being the same yesterday,

The word for wheel in Hebrew is ‘owphan and it is from an unused root meaning to revolve - the wheel it can also mean "manner or way" It appears to me that this could be describing the way or course that the creatures are taking on the earth. One writer compared this wheel to the course or way that the Spirit works with us. "Providence, represented by the wheels, produces changes. Sometimes one spoke of the wheel is uppermost, sometimes another; but the motion of the wheel on its own axletree is regular and steady. We need not despond in adversity; the wheels are turning round and will raise us in due time, while those who presume in prosperity know not how soon they may be cast down." (Matthew Henry's Commentary) - That is there are ups and downs, like the wheel, in our lives.

Much of what Ezekiel said was figuratively because of the difficulty he had in conveying eternal things. Two statements he made about the wheels, however, give significant insight into the message he sought to convey. He said that "the spirit of the living creature (singular) was in the wheels" (vs. 20-2]). A more direct translation of the Hebrew words used there would render "the spirit of the life (or the spirit of life) was in the wheels". He also said that "their rings [rim top or extent of their curve "were so high that they were dreadful" hey were very lofty, or there extent was great and... were full of eyes" (vs. 18). The prophet Joseph Smith, in explaining Rev. 4: 6-8 said that the eyes of the beasts mentioned there "are a representation of light and knowledge, that is, they are full of knowledge" (D&C 77:4). Thus, the spirit of life was in the wheels, their extent was exceedingly great, and they were full of knowledge. These characteristics closely parallel the description the Lord gave in. the Doctrine and Covenants 88:6-13, of the spirit of Christ which fills the immensity of space. With this in mind, note the following comparison:
This verse could have been translated: "And a likeness was over the heads of the living creature, an expanse, like the color of awesome crystal, stretched out over their heads from above."
voice of the Almighty, the voice of speech, as the noise of an host: when they stood, they let down their wings.
